chunk_summary: This section discusses hypernatremia risk in children, emphasizing safe intravenous fluid practices, including isotonic solutions, gradual sodium correction, and strict monitoring protocols.
learning_objectives: Recognize clinical signs of hypernatremic dehydration in children.
Understand safe sodium correction rates to prevent complications like cerebral edema.
Apply guidelines for maintenance intravenous fluid therapy using isotonic solutions.
Monitor electrolyte levels and adjust fluid therapy in high-risk pediatric scenarios.
key_facts: Hypernatremia correction should reduce serum sodium by no more than 0.5 mmol/L per hour or 10–12 mmol/L per day.
Maintenance IV fluids for children >28 days should use 0.9% saline with dextrose; potassium is added only after urine output is established.
Calculate maintenance fluid volume using the Holliday–Segar method: 4 mL/kg/hour for first 10 kg, 2 mL/kg/hour for next 10 kg, 1 mL/kg/hour thereafter.
High-risk situations for electrolyte monitoring include pneumonia, bronchiolitis, meningitis, postoperative states, and ICU care.
Neonates and preterm infants require specialized protocols due to different renal physiology.
reference_quote: AAP and NICE guidance supports 0.9% saline-based maintenance with dextrose; add potassium only after urine output is established.
document_key_topics: Electrolyte monitoring in high-risk pediatric cases
